explicit_text: I'm from Georgetown, Kentucky, and I still live there now. After finishing my doctoral work, I moved back to Georgetown to be closer to my family when my dad was sick. While living there, I got involved in local food justice efforts, including organizing a community garden in partnership with local churches and the college. My wife and I even sold our house and moved in with family to simplify our lives and focus on this work.
implicit_text: I'm from a small city in Kentucky, and I still live there now. After finishing my doctoral work, I moved back to this hometown to be closer to my family when my dad was sick. While living there, I got involved in local food justice efforts, including organizing a community garden in partnership with local churches and the nearby college. My wife and I even sold our house and moved in with family to simplify our lives and focus on this work.

explicit_text: Lexington is just across town from Georgetown, and I went to Transylvania University there for my undergraduate studies in philosophy. I also taught at Transylvania for several years after returning to Kentucky. Lexington is where I experienced some of the 4th of July celebrations with their loud fireworks, which can be alienating to me. It’s a place closely tied to my academic and early professional life.
implicit_text: The city just across town from my hometown is where I went for my undergraduate studies in philosophy at a well-known liberal arts university. I also taught there for several years after returning to Kentucky. This city is where I experienced some of the 4th of July celebrations with their loud fireworks, which can be alienating to me. It’s a place closely tied to my academic and early professional life.

explicit_text: I attended Transylvania University in Lexington for my undergraduate degree, majoring in philosophy. Later, after moving back to Kentucky, I taught in the philosophy department there for two years and then served as associate dean for religious life for about four or five years. During that time, I developed a model connecting academic work with community engagement around food justice and faith. Eventually, differences in vision led me to leave the university to focus more fully on climate and food justice activism.

During that time, I developed a model connecting academic work with community engagement around food justice and faith. explicit_text: I went to divinity school in Nashville, Tennessee, which was an important step in my academic and spiritual formation. This experience helped shape my theological perspective before I pursued doctoral work. Nashville was a significant place in my educational journey before I moved on to Vanderbilt University and then Syracuse University.
implicit_text: I went to divinity school in a major Tennessee city known for its music scene, which was an important step in my academic and spiritual formation. This experience helped shape my theological perspective before I pursued doctoral work. That city was a significant place in my educational journey before I moved on to graduate studies at a prominent university in Nashville and then to Syracuse University.

explicit_text: After divinity school in Nashville, I continued my studies at Vanderbilt University. This was part of my academic path before I pursued doctoral work at Syracuse University. Vanderbilt played a role in deepening my theological and philosophical understanding during that period.
implicit_text: After divinity school in the Tennessee city famous for country music, I continued my studies at a prestigious university located there. This was part of my academic path before I pursued doctoral work at Syracuse University. This university played a role in deepening my theological and philosophical understanding during that period.

explicit_text: I completed my doctoral work in philosophical theology at Syracuse University. Toward the end of my dissertation, I moved back to Georgetown due to my father's illness but finished my degree while living there. Syracuse was where I developed much of my scholarly foundation before transitioning into teaching and activism.
implicit_text: I completed my doctoral work in philosophical theology at a university in upstate New York. Toward the end of my dissertation, I moved back to my Kentucky hometown due to my father's illness but finished my degree while living there. This university was where I developed much of my scholarly foundation before transitioning into teaching and activism.

explicit_text: Green Christians is a small dinner church that I help convene, meeting in our house in Georgetown. We are committed to living out our faith through justice, joy, and resiliency, especially focusing on environmental and food justice issues. It’s a community where faith and activism intersect in tangible, joyful ways.

It’s a community where faith and activism intersect in tangible, joyful ways.

explicit_text: INCITE! is a collective of Black feminists whose book, The Revolution Will Not Be Funded, was recommended to me early on by a friend as I was thinking about organizing around food justice. Their critique helped me shift away from trying to start a nonprofit and instead focus on building networks and collective efforts. INCITE!'s work influenced my approach to activism and organizing.

cues: The Revolution Will Not Be Funded

explicit_text: Creation Justice Ministries is an ecumenical hub organization that connects progressive and mainline Christian denominations around climate issues. I’m involved with them in planning regional retreats for pastors focused on climate justice, including one upcoming retreat in Columbus, Ohio. This work aims to build local networks and deepen relationships among faith leaders committed to environmental stewardship.
implicit_text: I’m involved with an ecumenical hub organization that connects progressive and mainline Christian denominations around climate issues. I help plan regional retreats for pastors focused on climate justice, including one upcoming retreat in Columbus, Ohio. This work aims to build local networks and deepen relationships among faith leaders committed to environmental stewardship.

explicit_text: The Poor People's Campaign is a model for community organizing that inspires some of the work I do with Creation Justice Ministries. Like the campaign, the retreats and gatherings I help organize aim to mobilize people and build community connections around justice issues. It’s about reversing the usual order by starting with community organizing to create sustained movements.
implicit_text: A well-known community organizing campaign focused on poverty and justice inspires some of the work I do with the ecumenical climate-focused ministry. Like that campaign, the retreats and gatherings I help organize aim to mobilize people and build community connections around justice issues. It’s about reversing the usual order by starting with community organizing to create sustained movements.

explicit_text: Columbus, Ohio, is the location for an upcoming Creation Justice Ministries retreat this October that I’m helping to plan. This retreat will bring together people from Kentucky and Ohio to build networks focused on climate and faith-based justice work. It’s an important regional gathering to strengthen local connections and collaborative efforts.
implicit_text: A Midwestern city in Ohio is the location for an upcoming regional retreat this October that I’m helping to plan. This retreat will bring together people from Kentucky and Ohio to build networks focused on climate and faith-based justice work. It’s an important regional gathering to strengthen local connections and collaborative efforts.

explicit_text: I’m involved with the Westar Institute, which is an organization that brings together scholars of religion to conduct innovative research. I chair a new seminar there called the Christ Seminar, focusing on 'Christologies of the people,' exploring diverse and often marginalized Christian traditions. Westar emphasizes public scholarship, and our work aims to reclaim progressive voices about Jesus and Christian identity.

I chair a new seminar there called the Christ Seminar, focusing on 'Christologies of the people,' exploring diverse and often marginalized Christian traditions. The institute emphasizes public scholarship, and our work aims to reclaim progressive voices about Jesus and Christian identity.

explicit_text: The Christ Seminar is a seminar I chair through the Westar Institute that focuses on 'Christologies of the people.' We bring together international scholars, many of whom are people of color, to explore and center marginalized Christian traditions. Our goal is to do public work that highlights progressive and leftist perspectives on who Jesus is and what it means to follow him.
implicit_text: The seminar I chair through a religious studies institute focuses on 'Christologies of the people.' We bring together international scholars, many of whom are people of color, to explore and center marginalized Christian traditions. Our goal is to do public work that highlights progressive and leftist perspectives on who Jesus is and what it means to follow him.

explicit_text: Norman Wirzba is a scholar who used to teach at Georgetown and now teaches at Duke University. His work on the intersection of environment and theology was influential for me as I developed my passion for food and climate justice. We come from similar academic backgrounds, and conversations with him helped me grow intellectually in this field.
implicit_text: A scholar who used to teach at my Kentucky hometown university and now teaches at a prestigious university in North Carolina influenced me deeply. His work on the intersection of environment and theology was influential for me as I developed my passion for food and climate justice. We come from similar academic backgrounds, and conversations with him helped me grow intellectually in this field.

explicit_text: Duke University is where Norman Wirzba currently teaches, and his work there has influenced my thinking about the intersection of theology and environmental issues. While I didn’t study there myself, Duke represents an important academic context connected to my intellectual mentors. It’s part of the broader academic network that has shaped my approach to climate and food justice.

explicit_text: Jane Addams’ concept of the 'snare of preparation' deeply resonated with me in my teaching. She describes how raising awareness about social problems without providing avenues for action can lead to cynicism and apathy. This insight motivated me to connect my students to tangible, joyful practices like food justice, so they could engage meaningfully rather than feel stuck.

She describes how raising awareness about social problems without providing avenues for action can lead to cynicism and apathy. This insight motivated me to connect my students to tangible, joyful practices like food justice, so they could engage meaningfully rather than feel stuck.

explicit_text: I have been teaching at Lexington Theological Seminary for about six or seven years, but Wake Forest Divinity School came up in conversations about theological education. While I’m not directly affiliated with Wake Forest, it represents part of the broader landscape of divinity schools where issues of justice and faith intersect. My own work aligns with the kinds of progressive theological education that schools like Wake Forest promote.

My own work aligns with the kinds of progressive theological education that such schools promote.
